name: Cold Sores (Oral Herpes Simplex)

key_questions: Prodrome: tingling, burning, itching at site before vesicles appear (herpes-specific)?
Location: lip/perioral (cold sore) vs intraoral (aphthous ulcer)?
red_flags: Eye involvement (herpetic keratoconjunctivitis) → urgent ophthalmology (can cause blindness)
Eczema herpeticum: HSV + eczema → widespread vesicular eruption — emergency
Immunocompromised patient → disseminated herpes — systemic antiviral urgently
Herpes encephalitis: headache + fever + confusion after cold sore → emergency
ddx: Aphthous ulcers (intraoral, not on vermilion border, no prodrome)

first_line.name: Ontario MA Scope Note
first_line.notes: ⭐ Pharmacists can prescribe oral antivirals for herpes labialis under Ontario MA (added Oct 2023). Most effective when started at prodrome (tingling/itching) — hours matter.

first_line.name: Topical Antiviral / Barrier Agent
first_line.drugs: Docosanol 10% cream (Abreva) q2–3h while awake × 5 days (OTC) | Acyclovir 5% cream (Zovirax) 5× daily × 4 days
first_line.notes: Best effect when applied at the PRODROME stage (tingling) before vesicles appear. Docosanol: OTC, blocks viral entry into cells. Acyclovir cream: modestly reduces duration by ~0.5–1 day. All topical agents: modest efficacy — reduce healing time by 1–2 days. Start at first symptom. Note: Penciclovir 1% cream (Denavir/Vectavir) is no longer marketed in Canada — do not recommend.
first_line.preferred_when: Mild-moderate cold sore, infrequent recurrence, patient declines oral antiviral

second_line.name: Oral Antiviral — Episodic Treatment (moderate-severe or frequent recurrence)
second_line.drugs: Valacyclovir (Valtrex) 2 g BID × 1 day (single-day regimen — most convenient) | Valacyclovir 500 mg BID × 3 days (alternative episodic regimen) | Acyclovir 400 mg 5× per day × 5 days (every 4 hours while awake) OR Acyclovir 200 mg 5× per day × 5 days | Famciclovir 1500 mg single dose
second_line.notes: Oral antivirals significantly more effective than topical — reduce healing by 1–2 days and reduce pain. Best if taken at prodrome (tingling/itching) — timing is critical. Valacyclovir 2 g BID × 1 day: most convenient (single-day). Acyclovir correct episodic dose for herpes labialis: 400 mg 5× daily OR 200 mg 5× daily × 5 days (NOT 400 mg TID — that is the genital HSV suppressive dose). Renal dose adjustment required for acyclovir and valacyclovir if CrCl <50 mL/min — reduce dose/frequency. Pregnancy: acyclovir and valacyclovir are generally considered safe in pregnancy (category B equivalent) — benefit usually outweighs risk.

second_line.name: Suppressive Therapy (frequent recurrence ≥6 outbreaks/year with significant impact)
second_line.drugs: Valacyclovir 500 mg OD (preferred) | Acyclovir 400 mg BID (alternative)
second_line.notes: Consider for patients with ≥6 outbreaks/year or significant psychosocial impact. Reduces recurrence frequency by ~70–80%. Annual reassessment to determine if ongoing suppression still needed. Renal dose adjustment required if CrCl <50 mL/min.

non_pharm: Apply at first sign of tingling/prodrome — most effective intervention is early treatment
Keep lesion clean and dry; avoid picking/squeezing
Avoid kissing and sharing utensils/lip products during active outbreak
Sun protection (lip balm with SPF) — UV exposure is a common trigger
L-lysine 1 g OD (supplement) — low-quality evidence; some patients report benefit for reducing recurrence frequency; generally safe
refer_when: First episode with severe or atypical lesions
Recurrent cold sores ≥6/year — consider suppressive therapy (pharmacist can initiate under Ontario MA)
Failure to respond to standard antiviral therapy — consider acyclovir resistance (immunocompromised)

references: Ontario College of Pharmacists. Minor Ailments: Cold Sores (Herpes Labialis). OCP Prescribing Standards, O. Reg. 256/24 (2025).
RxTx (Canadian Pharmacists Association). Herpes Labialis — Therapeutic Choices, 8th ed. (2023).
Workowski KA, et al. Sexually Transmitted Infections Treatment Guidelines (includes HSV). CDC/MMWR. 2021;70(4):1–187.
NICE. Herpes simplex — oral: Clinical Knowledge Summary (CKS). National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (2023).
